items.description: A user-facing capability that solves a specific problem

fields.description: Human-readable summary of the feature
fields.name: description

fields.description: The user problem this feature addresses
fields.name: problem

fields.description: How this feature solves the stated problem
fields.name: solution

fields.description: Importance level: critical, high, medium, or low
fields.name: priority

fields.description: Lifecycle state: proposed, accepted, in_progress, done, deferred, or deprecated
fields.name: status

fields.description: Criteria that must be met for the feature to be considered complete
fields.name: acceptance

fields.description: Other features that must be completed before this one
fields.name: depends_on

fields.description: Related features referenced by this feature
fields.name: features

fields.description: External references such as issues, URLs, or documents
fields.name: refs

fields.description: Justification for the current status or a status change
fields.name: reason

fields.description: Person or team responsible for this feature
fields.name: owner

fields.description: Additional people or teams contributing to this feature
fields.name: contributors

fields.description: T-shirt size estimate: xs, s, m, l, or xl
fields.name: effort

items.inference_guide: Look for user-facing capabilities described in product documents, issue trackers, or feature flag configurations. Signals: feature flag definitions; product requirement documents; user stories in README or docs/; GitHub issues labeled 'feature' or 'enhancement'; CHANGELOG entries describing new capabilities; marketing copy describing what users can do. Extract the problem (user pain point) and solution (how the feature addresses it). Fill acceptance with testable completion criteria (e.g., 'User can export CSV in <2s'). Set priority and status from issue labels or project boards. Link depends_on for prerequisite features. Features are user-facing capabilities; for internal technical units of work, use behavior instead. Skip: internal technical improvements, bug fixes, refactoring work.
items.name: Feature

items.description: A user journey through the product experience

fields.description: The user archetype who undertakes this journey
fields.name: persona

fields.description: Human-readable summary of the journey
fields.name: description

fields.description: Communication or distribution channels used in this journey
fields.name: channels

fields.description: Features exercised during this journey
fields.name: features

fields.description: Ordered steps the user takes through the journey
fields.name: flow

fields.description: Importance level: critical, high, medium, or low
fields.name: priority

items.inference_guide: Look for user workflows, onboarding flows, tutorial sequences, and end-to-end scenarios described in docs or UX artifacts. Signals: user flow diagrams; onboarding guides; tutorial/walkthrough documentation; e2e test scenarios that follow a user path; README 'Getting Started' sections describing multi-step workflows. Each journey should reference a persona and list flow steps in order. Link features exercised during the journey. Link channels used (web, CLI, API). Skip: single-action interactions (those are behaviors), internal system workflows, developer-only processes.
items.name: Journey

items.description: A shippable unit of work with dependencies and milestones

fields.description: Human-readable summary of the deliverable
fields.name: description

fields.description: Kind of artifact produced: cli, service, library, web_app, mobile_app, api, extension, documentation, or package
fields.name: artifact_type

fields.description: Lifecycle state: draft, in_progress, shipped, or deprecated
fields.name: status

fields.description: User journeys this deliverable supports
fields.name: journeys

fields.description: Modules included in this deliverable
fields.name: modules

fields.description: Semantic version of this deliverable
fields.name: version

fields.description: Milestones this deliverable is tracked under
fields.name: milestones

fields.description: Other deliverables that must ship before this one
fields.name: depends_on

fields.description: Justification for the current status or a status change
fields.name: reason

fields.description: Person or team responsible for this deliverable
fields.name: owner

fields.description: Additional people or teams contributing to this deliverable
fields.name: contributors

items.inference_guide: Look for independently shippable artifacts: binaries, packages, services, libraries, or documentation bundles. Signals: Cargo.toml/package.json with distinct package names; Dockerfile definitions; CI/CD deployment targets; published npm/crate packages; separate apps in a monorepo (apps/ directory); versioned API specifications. Set artifact_type (cli, service, library, web_app, api, etc.). Link modules it contains and milestones it's tracked under. Link depends_on for build or runtime dependencies between deliverables. Skip: internal modules (those are modules), test utilities, development-only tooling.
items.name: Deliverable

items.description: A significant project checkpoint with tracked progress

fields.description: Human-readable summary of the milestone
fields.name: description

fields.description: Lifecycle state: planned, in_progress, completed, or blocked
fields.name: status

fields.description: Features that must be delivered to complete this milestone
fields.name: features

fields.description: Conditions that must be met to consider the milestone complete
fields.name: exit_criteria

fields.description: Planned completion date for this milestone
fields.name: target_date

fields.description: Date when work on this milestone began or is planned to begin
fields.name: start_date

fields.description: Modules scoped to this milestone
fields.name: modules

fields.description: Other milestones that must be completed before this one
fields.name: depends_on

fields.description: Outstanding issues preventing progress on this milestone
fields.name: blockers

fields.description: Importance level: critical, high, medium, or low
fields.name: priority

fields.description: Justification for the current status or a status change
fields.name: reason

fields.description: Person or team responsible for this milestone
fields.name: owner

fields.description: Additional people or teams contributing to this milestone
fields.name: contributors

fields.description: External references such as issues, URLs, or documents
fields.name: refs

items.inference_guide: Look for project phases, release planning checkpoints, and grouped sets of work with target dates. Signals: GitHub milestones; project board columns representing phases; roadmap documents with dated targets; sprint/iteration boundaries; 'Phase 1/2/3' labels; release planning documents. Extract target_date, exit_criteria, and status. Link features that must be delivered and modules scoped to this milestone. Link depends_on for sequential milestone ordering. Skip: individual tasks (those are features or behaviors), recurring ceremonies, ongoing maintenance work.
items.name: Milestone

items.description: A logical grouping of related features and behaviors

fields.description: Category or family this module belongs to
fields.name: family

fields.description: Human-readable summary of the module
fields.name: description

fields.description: Features contained within this module
fields.name: features

fields.description: Other modules this module depends on
fields.name: depends_on

fields.description: Justification for this module's existence or grouping
fields.name: reason

items.inference_guide: Look for logical boundaries in the codebase: bounded contexts, packages, crates, or top-level directories that group related functionality. Signals: top-level directories in src/ (e.g., src/auth/, src/billing/); Cargo workspace members; Go packages; separate npm workspaces; DDD bounded contexts; module declarations in architecture docs. Set family for higher-level grouping (e.g., 'core', 'platform', 'integration'). Link features contained in this module and depends_on for inter-module dependencies. Modules are logical groupings of features; for shippable artifacts, use deliverable instead. Skip: pure utility directories with no domain cohesion, test directories, build tooling.
items.name: Module

items.description: A glossary entry defining domain-specific vocabulary

fields.description: The precise meaning of this term in the project's domain
fields.name: definition

fields.description: Where or how this term is typically used
fields.name: context

fields.description: Alternative names or abbreviations for this term
fields.name: aliases

fields.description: Related terms for cross-referencing
fields.name: see_also

fields.description: The module (bounded context) that owns this term's definition
fields.name: module

items.inference_guide: Look for domain-specific vocabulary that team members need to agree on. Signals: glossary sections in docs; comments explaining domain jargon; type/struct names that embed domain concepts; README sections defining terminology; onboarding docs explaining project vocabulary; code comments like 'in our context, X means...'. The definition field should be precise and unambiguous. Add aliases for abbreviations or alternative names. Link see_also for related terms and module for the bounded context that owns the term. Skip: standard programming terms, framework concepts, widely-known acronyms.
items.name: Term

items.description: A user archetype representing a target audience segment

fields.description: Human-readable summary of this persona
fields.name: description

fields.description: Technical proficiency of this persona (e.g., beginner, intermediate, expert)
fields.name: technical_level

fields.description: What this persona wants to achieve with the product
fields.name: goals

fields.description: Frustrations or problems this persona currently faces
fields.name: pain_points

fields.description: Whether this persona is active or deprecated
fields.name: status

fields.description: Justification for the current status or a status change
fields.name: reason

fields.description: Features this persona cares about most
fields.name: key_features

items.inference_guide: Look for distinct user archetypes referenced in product docs, user stories, or access control systems. Signals: role-based access control definitions (admin, editor, viewer); user story formats ('As a <persona>...'); docs/personas/ directory; marketing segments; README describing who the product is for; distinct CLI/API user types. Extract technical_level, goals, and pain_points. Link key_features this persona cares about. One persona per distinct user archetype with meaningfully different needs. Skip: system/service accounts, test users, internal roles that don't represent real users.
items.name: Persona

items.description: A communication or distribution channel for the product

fields.description: Human-readable summary of this channel
fields.name: description

fields.description: How users interact through this channel (e.g., sync, async, push, pull)
fields.name: interaction_model

fields.description: URL or endpoint for this channel
fields.name: url

fields.description: Whether this channel is active or deprecated
fields.name: status

fields.description: Justification for the current status or a status change
fields.name: reason

items.inference_guide: Look for distinct surfaces through which users interact with the product. Signals: separate frontend applications (web app, mobile app, CLI); API endpoints exposed to external consumers; notification channels (email, push, SMS); documentation sites; IDE extensions; webhook integrations; Slack/Discord bots. Set interaction_model (sync, async, push, pull). Set url if applicable. One channel per distinct interaction surface. Skip: internal communication between services (those are ports), monitoring/alerting channels, CI/CD pipelines.
items.name: Channel

items.description: A versioned product release bundling deliverables

fields.description: Semantic version identifier for this release

fields.description: Deliverables included in this release

fields.name: milestones

fields.description: Planned release date

fields.description: Actual date the release shipped

fields.description: Summary of changes included in this release
fields.name: changelog

fields.name: depends_on

fields.description: Person or team responsible for this release
fields.name: owner

fields.description: Additional people or teams contributing to this release
fields.name: contributors

fields.description: Justification for the current status or a status change
fields.name: reason

fields.description: External references such as issues, URLs, or documents
fields.name: refs

items.inference_guide: Look for versioned release artifacts, changelogs, and release planning documents. Signals: CHANGELOG.md entries; GitHub releases; git tags following semver; release branches (release/v*); Cargo.toml/package.json version fields; release notes documents; deployment manifests with version numbers. Set version (semver), target_date, and status. Link deliverables included and milestones completed by this release. Skip: pre-release/nightly builds (unless formally tracked), hotfix patches (unless they warrant a release entity), internal development versions.
items.name: Release
